title: General AI Literacy
audience: Anyone who wants a solid practical understanding of AI at work.

questions.text: What is the clearest definition of AI in a workplace context?
options: Software that performs tasks using patterns, prediction, or language reasoning.
Software hosted in the cloud with dashboards and automated notifications.
A robot or script that removes people from every operational workflow.
A spreadsheet tool that runs macros and stores business calculations.

questions.text: What does a large language model (LLM) primarily do?
options: Indexes company files so they can be retained and retrieved on request.
Predicts likely token sequences to produce or transform language.
Checks every answer against verified databases before responding.
Encrypts network traffic between browsers, servers, and APIs.

questions.text: What is a hallucination in AI output?
options: A deliberately deceptive answer generated after the system detects risk.
A response where sentence structure is broken but the facts are reliable.
A fluent response that includes incorrect or unsupported claims.
A delayed response caused by network latency or overloaded servers.

questions.text: Which prompt usually improves response quality most?
options: Do this task quickly and make the answer useful for whoever reads it.
Write a useful summary of the content and keep the wording professional.
You know the general idea, so make reasonable assumptions and continue.
Act as a project analyst; give 5 executive bullets with risks and actions.

questions.text: What is the best first step before using AI output in a real decision?
options: Publish it as-is once the answer sounds confident and well structured.
Check the key facts, assumptions, sources, and decision impact.
Translate the response into a second language to reveal inconsistencies.
Improve formatting and tone before sharing it with decision makers.

questions.text: Which is safest to avoid sharing in public AI tools unless approved?
options: Personal, customer, or confidential company information.
Public product descriptions already approved for external use.
Published blog text copied from the organisation's website.
Generic meeting agenda templates with no sensitive details.

questions.text: What does 'human-in-the-loop' mean?
options: A person writes all AI-assisted content manually from start to finish.
A person reviews or approves outputs at defined control points.
People are removed once the AI workflow reaches production use.
Only managers can operate the tool or approve generated prompts.

questions.text: Which use case is usually low risk and high value to start with?
options: Automating legal sign-off with no human review or escalation path.
Diagnosing medical conditions for customers without clinician review.
Drafting summaries, emails, and first-pass working documents.
Approving supplier payments end-to-end without business controls.

questions.text: What is a practical way to reduce hallucinations?
options: Ask for structured output and require evidence or sources where possible.
Use all-caps instructions so the model treats the request as urgent.
Always make prompts longer, even when the extra detail is irrelevant.
Turn off spelling checks so the model focuses only on factual content.

questions.text: Why is prompt context important?
options: It lets the model use cheaper infrastructure for routine workplace tasks.
It gives the model the audience, intent, constraints, and success criteria.
It permanently updates the model's training data after every request.
It automatically converts every answer into valid JSON or spreadsheet data.

questions.text: What is a sign an AI workflow needs governance?
options: It influences financial, legal, customer, safety, or policy outcomes.
It produces polished charts that senior stakeholders may want to reuse.
It responds quickly enough to be embedded into a daily team routine.
It includes keyboard shortcuts or templates that make prompts faster.

questions.text: Which metric matters most for business AI adoption?
options: Total prompt volume across users, teams, tools, and departments.
How often AI is discussed in leadership forums and project meetings.
Measurable outcome improvement in quality, speed, risk, or cost.
Number of AI tools licensed, installed, configured, and promoted.

questions.text: What is retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) designed to do?
options: Retrieve relevant source material before generating a grounded answer.
Replace document platforms by storing every file inside the language model.
Increase randomness so repeated prompts produce more varied responses.
Hide model limits by making the generated response sound more confident.

questions.text: In practical terms, what is model drift?
options: When a deployed model is moved between folders, tenants, or cloud regions.
When performance degrades as real-world data or context changes.
When user writing styles change and prompts become less consistent.
When prompts are too short to include role, task, and output details.

questions.text: Which is the best way to use AI for drafting comms?
options: Send the first draft directly when the message is internally generated.
Use AI for a draft, then review tone, facts, approvals, and audience fit.
Limit AI to headings and subject lines so factual risk stays minimal.
Skip review for low-effort messages so speed benefits are preserved.

questions.text: What should you do when AI confidence seems high but evidence is weak?
options: Trust the confident wording if the response is clear and well structured.
Increase the urgency in the prompt and publish once the tone improves.
Ask for verification steps, sources, assumptions, or independent checks.
Repeat the same prompt with stronger wording until the answer stabilises.

questions.text: Which statement about AI and jobs is most accurate?
options: AI will immediately replace most knowledge work without role redesign.
AI changes task mixes, often augmenting roles and shifting skill needs.
AI mainly affects software engineering and leaves business roles unchanged.
AI has little workflow impact unless teams build their own models.

questions.text: What is an effective way to build AI capability in teams?
options: A one-off announcement email explaining that approved tools are available.
A tool rollout with account access but few examples or workflow changes.
Use-case playbooks, practical training, support, and measurable pilots.
Blocking experimentation until every possible governance issue is solved.

questions.text: When should an AI output be treated as high risk?
options: When it affects policy, legal exposure, money, safety, or commitments.
When it contains bullet points that could be reused in presentations.
When it is shorter than expected and may require additional context.
When it references meeting notes from a recent internal discussion.

questions.text: What is a mature AI operating model aiming for?
options: Maximum novelty and adoption speed, even if controls mature later.
Minimal experimentation until vendor and regulatory uncertainty disappears.
Central approval for every prompt, output, pilot, and user workflow.
Balanced speed, quality, safety, accountability, and clear ownership.
